Glossary About
East Asian Traditions

Fazang

643 – 712

Fazang 法藏, also romanized Fa-tsang, 643–712, is best treated not as a “philosopher of mind” in the post-Cartesian sense, but as a systematic Buddhist metaphysician whose work contains one of the most ambitious premodern accounts of mind, world, self, appearance, error, and transformation.

Fazang 法藏, also romanized Fa-tsang, 643–712, is best treated not as a “philosopher of mind” in the post-Cartesian sense, but as a systematic Buddhist metaphysician whose work contains one of the most ambitious premodern accounts of mind, world, self, appearance, error, and transformation. His central claim, reconstructed in contemporary terms, is that mind is not an inner Cartesian theatre, not a private substance, not a brain state, and not a merely subjective stream of representations. “Mind” is the name for the nondual, dependently arisen, all-pervading field in which subject, object, body, world, ignorance, and awakening are differentiated without ever becoming ultimately separate. Huayan thought interprets emptiness as interdependence and draws on Madhyamaka, Yogācāra, and the Awakening of Faith in Mahāyāna; Fazang is one of the two most important Huayan figures, along with Zongmi.

The philosophy of mind that can be extracted from Fazang is therefore a nondual relational philosophy of consciousness. It is “mind-first” only in a special sense: not because empirical reality is produced by an individual subject, as in crude subjective idealism, but because the most basic structure of reality is “One Mind” 一心, identified with tathāgatagarbha, suchness, Buddha-nature, and the dharma-realm. This One Mind is prior to the division between individual consciousness and its objects, and prior to the distinction between mental and material phenomena. Fazang’s One Mind has two aspects: Suchness Mind, which is unproduced, unceasing, and free from discriminative cognition, and Samsara Mind, which arises and ceases through conditions and appears as purity and impurity.

A good modern label would be Huayan nondual mind-realism or relational noetic monism, provided that “monism” is not understood as a single substance in the Western metaphysical sense. Fazang’s system is monistic because there is ultimately one dharma-realm, one all-inclusive relational totality. It is not substance-monistic because every phenomenon, including mind, is empty of self-nature. It is idealist-like because mind has ontological priority. It is not Berkeleyan idealism because “mind” is not a collection of finite perceivers plus divine perceiver. It is phenomenological because it analyzes how the world appears under delusion and awakening. It is not merely descriptive phenomenology because its goal is liberation. It is metaphysics, epistemology, psychology, ethics, and contemplative practice at once.

1. Historical position: Fazang in the global development of mind-theory

Fazang stands at the high point of Tang-dynasty Chinese Buddhism. He was a monk, translator, systematizer, ritual specialist, court intellectual, and Huayan patriarch. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes him as one of the foremost figures of medieval Chinese Buddhism, living among such major contemporaries as Xuanzang, Shenxiu, and Daoxuan. Under Empress Wu Zetian, Fazang’s Huayan teaching also became politically consequential: he lectured to the court, participated in major translation projects, and helped elevate the Avataṃsaka Sūtra as the supreme Buddhist scripture in his doctrinal hierarchy.

Historically, Fazang’s place in the philosophy of mind is unusual. In the Western canon, “philosophy of mind” often becomes sharply defined after the rise of mechanistic science and Descartes: the central problem becomes how mind and body, or mental and physical properties, relate. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes the mind-body problem as the problem of explaining how beings can have physical properties such as size, weight, shape, and motion, while also having mental properties such as perception, emotion, belief, desire, agency, subjecthood, and selfhood. The classic Western options become materialism, idealism, and dualism.

Fazang’s system develops in a very different problem-space. The central problem is not “How can nonphysical mind interact with physical body?” but “How does deluded subject-object experience arise within a nondual reality, and how can cognition be transformed so that this reality is seen without obstruction?” Buddhist thought begins from dukkha, no-self, dependent arising, and liberation; Huayan radicalizes these into an ontology of total interpenetration. The Stanford Huayan entry summarizes early Buddhist teaching as diagnosing suffering through the illusory belief in persistent selves and identifying enlightenment with full realization of no-self, which gives rise to universal compassion.

Fazang inherits several Indian and Chinese Buddhist streams. From Madhyamaka, he inherits emptiness: nothing possesses independent self-nature. From Yogācāra, he inherits mind-only analysis, the three natures, storehouse consciousness, and the critique of subject-object duality. From tathāgatagarbha thought and the Awakening of Faith, he inherits the doctrine of One Mind with two aspects: suchness and birth-and-death. From Chinese Buddhism, he inherits panjiao 判教, doctrinal classification, the practice of arranging apparently divergent teachings as partial levels of truth rather than mutually exclusive systems.

This gives Fazang a distinctive historical role. He is not simply an “Eastern idealist,” nor merely a Buddhist scholastic. He is one of the most rigorous architects of a mind-world ontology that tries to solve three problems at once: how appearances arise, how error is possible, and how enlightened cognition can see the same world differently without abolishing it. In this respect, he belongs beside figures such as Plotinus, Spinoza, Leibniz, Kant, Hegel, and contemporary anti-Cartesian philosophers as a major theorist of the relation between consciousness and reality, even though his conceptual vocabulary and soteriological aims differ sharply from theirs.

2. The textual and doctrinal basis

The most important Fazang-related materials for extracting a philosophy of mind are his Commentary on the Awakening of Faith in Mahāyāna 大乘起信論義記, his Treatise on the Five Teachings of Huayan 華嚴五教章, his Record of the Search for the Profundities of the Avataṃsaka-sūtra 華嚴經探玄記, and the traditionally associated Essay on the Golden Lion 金師子章. The IEP lists more than sixty works, commentaries, meditation manuals, and translation projects associated with Fazang, and identifies the Treatise on the Five Teachings as the central work in which he classifies Buddhist doctrines and places Huayan at the summit.

The Awakening of Faith is especially important for the philosophy of mind. It is an East Asian Buddhist treatise, probably composed in China in the sixth century, that synthesizes Yogācāra and tathāgatagarbha thought. It explains how mind can be the source of both enlightenment and ignorance by positing One Mind with two aspects: the absolute aspect, equivalent to tathāgatagarbha, and the phenomenal aspect, associated with ālayavijñāna, storehouse consciousness. Thought or nian 念 arises “suddenly” from One Mind, fracturing the world into conceptual phenomena; yet this ignorance does not stain true nature.

Fazang does not merely cite the Awakening of Faith incidentally. A 2024 study of Fazang’s Tanxuanji notes that he cites the Awakening of Faith thirty-six times there, and that his interpretation of the text shaped his understanding of bodhicitta, faith, and the Huayan path.

The Golden Lion is also crucial because it gives a compact metaphor for Fazang’s ontology. A golden lion statue appears as a lion with parts: mane, claws, teeth, eyes. Yet every part is gold. The lion is not nothing, because it appears and functions conventionally; but it is not independently real, because no lion exists apart from the gold. In Huayan terms, gold corresponds to principle, emptiness, or suchness; lion corresponds to phenomena, form, or events. The metaphor models the relation between ultimate and conventional reality, but also the relation between perceptual appearance and its nondual ground.

3. Fazang’s core metaphysics: the world as interpenetrating mind-field

Fazang’s metaphysics begins from the Huayan thesis that every phenomenon is interdependent with every other phenomenon. The classical metaphor is Indra’s net: at every node of the net is a jewel, and each jewel reflects all the others. The point is not merely poetic. It says that the identity of any thing is constituted by its relations to all other things. A phenomenon is not first self-contained and then externally related; relation is internal to its being.

In Fazang’s more technical formulation, each dharma includes all others, is included within all others, determines the identity of all others, and has its own identity determined by all others. He analyzes this both in terms of “different substance,” where each dharma relates to all other dharmas, and “same substance,” where each dharma relates to the totality of the dharma-realm. Every dharma simultaneously has the aspect of existing and the aspect of being empty; every dharma simultaneously has power and lacks power.

This is already a philosophy of mind in embryo. If every dharma is internally related to every other dharma, then mental states cannot be private, atomistic items contained inside a subject. A perception, desire, memory, or thought has its identity through the entire network of conditions: body, language, karma, world, social relations, past acts, present attention, and ultimate suchness. Fazang’s mereology is therefore also a theory of mental individuation. A mental event is not what it is by being sealed inside an interior subject; it is what it is through its position in the total dharmic field.

The second major metaphysical claim is One Mind. In his Commentary on the Awakening of Faith, Fazang identifies One Mind with tathāgatagarbha. But this is not an individual consciousness and not something opposed to matter. It is “the one and only source of all that exists,” prior to individual consciousness and prior to the objects of consciousness. This priority is ontological, not temporal: One Mind is the structural ground from which subject-object and mental-material distinctions arise.

Hamar’s study of nature-origination explains the same point in Huayan terms. Nature-origination is central to Huayan thought and is tied to the Avataṃsaka chapter on the manifestation of the Tathāgata, but its philosophical meaning is heavily shaped by the Awakening of Faith. Hamar notes that Fazang identifies mind-as-suchness with the Absolute and mind-subject-to-birth-and-death with the phenomenal, and treats their unobstructed interaction as ālayavijñāna, which is also identified with tathāgatagarbha and said to give rise to all dharmas.

The danger here is reification. If One Mind is called “source,” it can sound like a Buddhist God, a cosmic soul, or a metaphysical substance. Fazang’s system resists that reading. One Mind is suchness precisely as empty, nondual, and nonseparate from phenomena. It is not an entity behind the world. It is the world’s nondual mode of being, seen without the error of self-nature. The “source” is not outside its manifestations; gold is not somewhere behind the lion. The gold is the lion, and yet the lion has no independent essence apart from gold.

4. The two aspects of mind: Suchness Mind and Samsara Mind

The decisive move in Fazang’s philosophy of mind is the two-aspect analysis. One Mind has Suchness Mind and Samsara Mind. Suchness Mind is free from discriminating cognition, neither produced nor destroyed, and not captured by the oppositions of “this” and “that.” Samsara Mind arises and ceases according to conditions, and transforms into purity and impurity. Fazang further distinguishes Suchness as unchanging and Suchness as accompanying change, while Ignorance is both empty and actively functioning.

This provides a solution to a problem that runs through Buddhist mind-theory: if reality is fundamentally pure, how can ignorance arise? And if ignorance is real, how can enlightenment be possible? Fazang’s answer is that Suchness and Ignorance are not two substances. Suchness has an unchanging aspect and a condition-accompanying aspect. Ignorance is empty in that its objects are illusory, but it also functions in that it produces conditioned appearances and defilements. Thus delusion has causal efficacy without independent essence.

In modern philosophy of mind terms, Fazang avoids both eliminativism and naive realism about delusion. He does not say that error simply does not exist. Error functions. It structures perception, affect, action, and suffering. But error does not possess ultimate self-nature. The deluded world is not an ontologically separate world; it is the same reality misperceived through discriminative cognition. This is why Huayan can say, following broader Mahāyāna logic, that samsara and nirvana are not two different ontological domains.

This is also why right practice does not mean annihilating conditioned dharmas. Fazang’s account implies that because Suchness is present within Samsara Mind, practice consists in attending to the Suchness aspect of conditioned phenomena, not in making the phenomena disappear.

5. The three natures and the structure of appearance

Fazang’s three-nature theory is another crucial piece. Yogācāra’s classical three natures are the imagined or fabricated nature, the dependent nature, and the perfected nature. In ordinary delusion, we take constructed appearances as independently real. In deeper analysis, we see that appearances depend on conditions. In perfected realization, we see that subject-object duality is empty. Yogācāra itself is not simple Western idealism: the Stanford entry warns that Yogācāra consciousness is not an individual or universal mental substance like Berkeley’s perceiving souls or Hegel’s absolute spirit, and that Yogācāra rejects subject-object duality at the ultimate level.

Fazang adapts the three natures by assigning each nature two aspects: one fundamental and one derivative. The fundamental aspects are pure and unchanging, lacking self-nature, and nonexistent from the standpoint of reality. The derivative aspects are condition-conforming, apparently existent as interdependent, and conventionally existent in common sense. His strategy is to include what is valid in both Yogācāra and Madhyamaka while avoiding their one-sidedness.

For philosophy of mind, this gives a powerful account of perception. What appears is not dismissed as nothing. It is dependently arisen and pragmatically operative. But its apparent self-sufficiency is fabricated. An object of perception is therefore neither a mind-independent substance nor a private mental image. It is a dependently arisen appearance whose reality lies in relational functioning and whose ultimate truth lies in emptiness. The perceived “lion” is not sheer illusion, but its lion-ness has no independent essence apart from the gold.

This differs from both naive realism and representationalism. Naive realism treats the object as simply there, with self-standing identity. Representationalism treats the immediate object as a mental content inside consciousness. Fazang treats subject, object, and their relation as co-arisen within a karmic, conceptual, and ontological field. The world is not denied, but its separateness is denied.

6. Consciousness, karmic appearance, and the birth of subject-object duality

The Awakening of Faith gives Fazang a layered analysis of mind. One scholarly reconstruction describes the text’s citta–manas–manovijñāna structure: citta parallels store consciousness, manas names several modes through which unenlightened mind begins to arise, and manovijñāna is associated with attachment to “I” and “mine” and with object-discriminating consciousness. Importantly, this is not merely epistemology; it is a cosmogonic scheme explaining how the delusive world comes into being, and it can be reversed soteriologically as a path from coarser to subtler appearances.

The key technical notion here is karmic appearance. In the Huayan interpretation, karmic appearance is extremely subtle because subject and object have not yet been distinguished. A later commentary describes it as not distinguishing subject and object; it gives birth to the delusive world in its initial movement. Yao’s study notes that many commentators focused on the threefold structure of karmic appearance, perceiving, and object, which resembles the Yogācāra structure of self-cognition, subject, and object. Fazang was the first to identify karmic appearance with self-cognition.

This is philosophically significant. Fazang’s account suggests that intentional consciousness—the familiar structure in which a subject is aware of an object—is not primitive. Before subject and object split, there is a subtler auto-affective or self-cognitive stirring. Only after that do perceiver and perceived separate. In modern terms, intentionality is derivative from a pre-dual field; the “aboutness” of consciousness is not the fundamental fact, but a conditioned articulation of a more basic nondual process.

Yao also notes a difference between Fazang and standard Yogācāra. In some Yogācāra accounts, self-cognition is the effect of cognition; for Fazang, karmic appearance is the cause of the delusive world and suffering within it. Thus Fazang shifts reflexive awareness from an epistemic feature of cognition to a cosmogonic and soteriological principle: the first movement of delusion becomes the root from which subject-object cognition unfolds.

7. Selfhood: no-self without nihilism

Fazang’s philosophy of mind is also a theory of self. The self is not a persisting substance. Ordinary selfhood is a product of discriminative consciousness, attachment, and karmic continuity. The “I” is conventionally functional but ultimately empty. This aligns with the Buddhist diagnosis that belief in persistent individual selves produces craving and suffering, and that enlightenment involves realizing no-self.

But Fazang is not a nihilist about persons. A sentient being is not an independent substance, but it is a real node of karmic and dharmic functioning. The person is like the lion-form: conventionally identifiable, causally efficacious, ethically significant, but empty of independent essence. This is why compassion follows from no-self rather than being undermined by it. If self and other are not ultimately sealed off from one another, then another’s suffering is not metaphysically alien to “me.”

This differs from Hume’s bundle theory, though there is convergence. Hume also denies that introspection reveals a substantial self, describing the self as a bundle of perceptions. But Hume’s conclusion is reached by an empiricist introspective analysis; Fazang’s no-self is embedded in a larger Buddhist account of emptiness, karma, interpenetration, and liberation. Hume deflates the self; Fazang deflates the self and then re-situates persons within an infinite web of ethical interdependence.

8. Mental causation and agency

Fazang’s system has no Cartesian interaction problem because it does not begin from two independent substances, mind and body. The Western problem asks how a nonextended thinking substance and an extended material substance can affect one another. Descartes identifies the essence of mind as thought and the essence of matter as extension; later Western philosophy inherits the problem of how these distinct domains relate.

For Fazang, mind and body are not two metaphysical kinds needing external linkage. They are dharmas within the same relational field. The more basic question is not interaction between mind and matter, but dependent arising among deluded and awakened modes of experience. Mental causation is karmic, relational, and holistic. A thought is not a private inner spark that then pushes matter; it is a conditioned event in a total network, simultaneously empty and functional.

Agency is similarly transformed. To act is not to express an autonomous ego, but to participate in dependent arising under conditions of ignorance or wisdom. Ignorant agency reinforces the subject-object-self structure. Awakened agency expresses non-obstruction, compassion, and skillful means. This is why bodhicitta, the thought of enlightenment, is so important. Fazang’s account of bodhicitta follows the Huayan idea that each element pervades all, so the perfection of one virtue involves the perfection of all; the first arousal of the thought of enlightenment is already linked to perfect enlightenment.

9. Epistemology: delusion, wisdom, and nonconceptual cognition

Fazang’s epistemology is not primarily about justified true belief. It is about how cognition can be distorted or liberated. Deluded cognition discriminates, objectifies, attaches, and treats conventionally useful distinctions as ultimately real. Wisdom does not destroy distinctions, but sees them as empty and mutually non-obstructing. The same lion can be seen as an independent lion or as gold. The difference is not sensory input alone; it is the structure of understanding.

This makes Fazang’s view strongly anti-reductionist about knowledge. Conceptual knowledge has a role, especially in doctrinal classification and philosophical argument, but ultimate realization is not achieved by conceptual proliferation. Fazang’s panjiao places the Sudden Teaching, associated with silence before ultimate reality, above more conceptual accounts; yet he places the Round Teaching of Huayan above even that, because Huayan does not merely fall silent before the ultimate but articulates how all phenomena interpenetrate without obstruction.

Thus Fazang’s philosophy of mind contains both apophatic and kataphatic tendencies. Apophatically, ultimate reality cannot be captured by discriminative language. Kataphatically, the structure of the dharma-realm can be explicated through metaphors, doctrinal classifications, and relational analyses. The Golden Lion, Indra’s Net, the rafter and building, and the six characteristics all function as cognitive therapies: they reorganize how the mind sees.

10. A systematic reconstruction of Fazang’s philosophy of mind

The following reconstruction is not a list of explicit propositions from Fazang, but a rigorous contemporary extraction from his Huayan framework.

First, mind is ontologically prior to the subject-object distinction. One Mind is not “my mind.” It is not consciousness as a personal stream. It is the nondual suchness from which individual consciousness and its objects are differentiated. This means that the ordinary opposition between mind and world is derivative.

Second, consciousness is stratified. At the deepest level is One Mind. At the level of two aspects are Suchness Mind and Samsara Mind. At the deluded level are karmic appearance, perceiving, object, manas, manovijñāna, attachment, conceptual discrimination, and suffering. At the liberated level, the same field is seen as suchness, emptiness, and non-obstruction.

Third, subject-object duality is a conditioned construction. It is not sheer illusion in the sense of nonexistence; it functions. But it is not ultimate. The perceiver and the perceived emerge together through karmic and conceptual articulation. This anticipates, in a Buddhist metaphysical mode, later anti-Cartesian accounts in which mind is not an isolated inner domain.

Fourth, mental content is holistic. A thought, perception, or desire is individuated by the entire dharma-realm, not merely by an inner psychological state. This follows from Fazang’s thesis that each dharma includes all others and has its identity determined by all others. In modern terms, Fazang is an extreme content externalist and relational holist, though his externalism is not merely social or environmental but cosmic and ontological.

Fifth, the self is conventional, karmic, and empty. There is no persisting ego-substance. But persons are not erased. They are dependently arisen loci of responsibility, suffering, practice, and compassion.

Sixth, the body is not a metaphysical opposite of mind. Bodies, sensations, gestures, rituals, environments, and thoughts all belong to the dharma-realm. Fazang does not offer neuroscience, but his framework dissolves the rigid inner/outer split that later shaped much Western philosophy of mind.

Seventh, error is functional emptiness. Ignorance lacks self-nature, but it causally functions. It structures experience and produces suffering. This is one of Fazang’s most subtle contributions: he can say that delusion is empty without saying it is inert or nonexistent.

Eighth, liberation is cognitive, affective, ethical, and ontological at once. Awakening is not merely acquiring new beliefs. It is a transformation in how reality appears, how attachment functions, how self-other relations are felt, and how action participates in the world.

11. Fazang and Western philosophy of mind: major convergences and divergences

Compared with Cartesian dualism, Fazang is radically non-Cartesian. Descartes’ mature ontology distinguishes mind, whose essence is thought, from matter, whose essence is extension. The resulting problem is how two radically different substances can be united in a human being and causally affect each other. Fazang’s system never generates this problem because mental and material phenomena are both dependently arisen dharmas within One Mind/dharmadhātu.

Compared with physicalism, Fazang is not reductive. Physicalism, in its slogan form, is the thesis that everything is physical; mind-brain identity theory holds that mental states and processes are identical with brain states and processes. Fazang does not deny conventional physical causality, but he denies that the physical has ultimate self-standing explanatory priority. The physical, like the mental, is empty, dependent, and interpretable only within the total dharma-realm.

Compared with functionalism, Fazang is surprisingly close in one respect and far in another. Functionalism says that a mental state is what it is by the role it plays in a system, especially its causal relations to stimuli, other mental states, and behavior. Fazang also individuates phenomena by relational role: each dharma is what it is through its relations to all others. But functionalism is usually a theory of mental states within cognitive systems; Fazang’s relationalism is an ontological, ethical, and soteriological theory of all phenomena.

Compared with Spinoza, Fazang shares an anti-dualist impulse. Spinoza treats mind and body as two expressions, under thought and extension, of one and the same thing, so the Cartesian mind-body problem does not technically arise. Fazang similarly dissolves substance dualism, but he does not posit one substance with attributes. His “one” is empty, relational, non-obstructive, and bound to the Buddhist path of liberation.

Compared with Leibniz, Fazang shows one of the closest Western parallels. Leibniz holds that each created substance perceives the entire universe, though mostly confusedly, and defines perception as the expression of many things in one. This resembles Indra’s Net, where each jewel reflects all jewels. But the difference is decisive: Leibnizian monads are simple substances, windowless and coordinated by pre-established harmony; Huayan dharmas are empty, mutually dependent, and non-obstructively interpenetrating. Leibniz’s system is theistic and substance-based; Fazang’s is non-theistic and emptiness-based.

Compared with Kant, Fazang also shares an interest in the conditions under which objects appear. Kant argues that space and time are forms of sensible intuition, that objects in space and time are appearances rather than things in themselves, and that we cannot cognize things in themselves. Fazang likewise denies that ordinary objects are known as self-subsistent things. But Fazang does not leave suchness as an unknowable noumenon. Suchness can be nonconceptually realized through awakening, and conventional phenomena themselves are the site where suchness is encountered.

Compared with Hegelian or absolute idealism, Fazang shares a concern with totality, the inadequacy of isolated finite objects, and the overcoming of subject-object opposition. Hegel’s idealism involves the identity of thinking and being and departs from Berkeley’s substance-based idealism; Fazang likewise cannot be reduced to Berkeleyan immaterialism. Yet Hegel’s development is dialectical, historical, and tied to Spirit; Fazang’s development is karmic, contemplative, and grounded in emptiness and dependent arising.

Compared with Plotinus, Fazang shares the idea of a supreme unity that exceeds ordinary conceptualization. Plotinus’ system centers on the One, Intellect, and Soul, and the One is an utterly simple first principle from which reality proceeds. Fazang’s One Mind can look similar, but Huayan is less hierarchical and more interpenetrative. In Plotinus, reality descends and ascends through hypostatic levels; in Fazang, the ultimate is not elsewhere but fully present in every phenomenon, as gold is present in every part of the lion.

Compared with modern externalism and enactivism, Fazang looks unexpectedly contemporary. Externalism says that what goes on in an individual’s mind is not always wholly determined by what is inside the body or brain, while enactivism treats cognition as emerging from sensorimotor activity across brain, body, and world. Fazang is more radical: the identity of any mental event depends not merely on environment or embodiment but on the total dharma-realm. Still, his rejection of the isolated inner mind strongly converges with anti-Cartesian currents in contemporary philosophy of mind and cognitive science.

12. Fazang and the “hard problem” of consciousness

Contemporary philosophy often treats consciousness as central because we need to explain conscious experience and its place in nature. The SEP entry on consciousness states that there is no agreed theory of consciousness, but there is broad consensus that an adequate account of mind requires understanding consciousness and how it relates to nonconscious reality.

Fazang reframes the problem. He is not primarily asking how matter produces subjective experience. He asks how deluded dualistic experience arises from nondual suchness, how ignorance can function without ultimate reality, and how experience can be transformed. This does not answer the contemporary hard problem in physicalist terms, but it exposes a different problem that modern philosophy often underplays: not “Why is there experience rather than no experience?” but “Why does experience appear as a world of independent objects and selves, and how can that appearance be undone?”

In this sense Fazang’s philosophy of mind is less a theory of qualia than a theory of appearance-formation. It explains how a world is disclosed through karmic, conceptual, affective, and ontological conditions. It also explains why the same world can be lived as samsara or nirvana depending on the mode of cognition. The transformation is not from one world to another, but from obstructed perception to non-obstructed perception.

13. Fazang’s strongest contributions to philosophy of mind

The first major contribution is the dissolution of the internalist picture of mind. Fazang gives a rigorous metaphysical basis for saying that mind is not inside the skull, not inside an ego, and not opposed to world. Mental events are world-involving because all dharmas are mutually determining.

The second contribution is his account of subject-object construction. Fazang identifies a pre-dual karmic appearance before perceiver and perceived are separated. This gives a deep account of intentionality as derivative rather than primitive. The “subject intending an object” is not the starting point; it is already a conditioned articulation.

The third contribution is his account of delusion as both empty and efficacious. This is philosophically valuable because many theories either reify illusion or trivialize it. Fazang does neither. Ignorance is not ultimately real, but it functions powerfully.

The fourth contribution is the integration of cognition and ethics. In Fazang, a false view of mind is not merely an intellectual mistake. It generates craving, self-other opposition, and suffering. Correct cognition is inseparable from compassion and bodhisattva practice.

The fifth contribution is his extreme holism. Every mental event is embedded in the whole. This is far more radical than ordinary semantic externalism, ecological psychology, or social theories of mind. It gives a metaphysical model in which no mental state is fully intelligible apart from the totality.

14. The main philosophical difficulties

The first difficulty is the origin of ignorance. If One Mind is suchness and Buddha-nature, why does ignorance arise at all? The Awakening of Faith says thought arises suddenly, and Fazang elaborates a two-aspect theory of Suchness and Ignorance. But a critic may argue that “sudden arising” names the problem rather than fully solving it. Fazang’s best answer is that ignorance is empty yet functional, and that Suchness accompanies conditions without being stained by them. Whether this is fully satisfying depends on whether one accepts nondual two-aspect metaphysics.

The second difficulty is the risk of reifying One Mind. Fazang wants to avoid both nihilism and eternalism. Yet the language of One Mind, source, tathāgatagarbha, and nature-origination can look like a substantial absolute. The strongest non-reifying reading is that One Mind names the empty, nondual, interpenetrating structure of reality rather than a cosmic subject or metaphysical substance.

The third difficulty is empirical under-specification. Fazang provides no neuroscientific account of perception, memory, affect, or embodiment. His theory can coexist with empirical cognitive science at the conventional level, but it does not replace empirical explanation. Its strength lies at the metaphysical, phenomenological, and soteriological levels.

The fourth difficulty is formal coherence. Claims that each dharma contains all dharmas, that each part includes the whole, and that each phenomenon is both empty and existent can appear contradictory. Fazang’s answer is that contradiction arises only if one assumes independent self-nature. Once phenomena are understood relationally and non-obstructively, the logic changes. Still, translating this into contemporary analytic metaphysics requires care.

The fifth difficulty is ineffability and argument. Fazang holds that ultimate reality exceeds discriminative cognition, yet he writes elaborate treatises about it. This is not simple inconsistency; Buddhist philosophy often uses concepts therapeutically to undo reification. But it raises a genuine methodological question: how can conceptual philosophy justify nonconceptual realization without converting it into another concept?

15. Final synthesis: the Fazangian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Fazang can be stated as follows.

Mind is not an entity among entities. Mind is the nondual suchness of the entire dharma-realm, appearing under conditions as the world of subject and object. Individual consciousness is a conditioned articulation of One Mind, not its owner or foundation. The ordinary mind mistakes dependently arisen appearances for self-subsistent things, and this mistake produces the lived world of samsara. The self is one such constructed appearance: conventionally useful, karmically operative, ethically significant, but empty of independent essence.

Perception is not passive reception of a mind-independent object, nor projection of a merely inner representation. Perception is a relational event in which subject, object, body, karma, language, affect, and world co-arise. Mental content is holistic because each dharma is internally related to all dharmas. Delusion is not unreal in the sense of nonfunctional; it is unreal in the sense of lacking self-nature. Awakening is the transformation of cognition by which phenomena are seen as empty, mutually inclusive, and non-obstructing. The world does not vanish; its separateness does.

In relation to Western philosophy, Fazang is closest to anti-Cartesian, holistic, idealist, and nondual traditions, but he is identical to none of them. He rejects Cartesian substance dualism before it ever arises. He is not a physicalist, because physicality has no ultimate privilege. He is not a Berkeleyan idealist, because One Mind is not a perceiving ego. He resembles Spinoza in dissolving the mind-body problem, Leibniz in seeing each part as reflecting the whole, Kant in analyzing appearance, Hegel in thinking totality, and enactivism/externalism in rejecting the isolated inner subject. But his governing concepts are Buddhist: emptiness, no-self, dependent arising, tathāgatagarbha, karma, bodhicitta, and liberation.

The importance of Fazang for philosophy of mind is therefore not that he anticipated one modern theory. It is that he offers a different architecture for the whole field. Instead of beginning with the individual subject and asking how it reaches the world, he begins with an interpenetrating world-mind field and asks how the illusion of isolated subjects and objects arises. That reversal is the heart of Fazang’s contribution.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
